topic: new rangers starting — mentoring the young ones

turns.content: Got two new rangers starting next week. Both young, just finished their training. My supervisor wants me to mentor them through the first couple of months. I want to do it well but I have not mentored anyone formally before.

It's good that you're thinking about how to approach it rather than just winging it. Do you know anything about their backgrounds — where they're from, what experience they have? And will they both be working alongside you on the same shifts?

One is from Darwin so she has some idea of the Top End. The other is from Melbourne, never been up this way before. They will both be on my patrol runs to start with until they learn this country. Very different starting points for the two of them.

That's a big difference. The Darwin one will have some baseline familiarity with the climate and environment, but the one from Melbourne is adjusting to everything at once — the heat, the remoteness, the wildlife, the landscape. You might need to pace things differently for each of them, at least in the early weeks.

That is what I was thinking. The training they did covers the textbook side of things. But there is another part that you cannot learn from a book. Reading the land, reading the water, knowing when the weather is about to change. That takes time and it takes being out there.

You could structure the first couple of weeks as observation — they ride along with you, watch how you work, learn the geography and the safety basics. Then in weeks three and four, start handing them specific tasks during patrols — data recording, site condition checks — while you supervise. Gradually increase their independence from there. For the observational skills you're describing, one approach that works well is asking questions rather than just explaining things. Something like "What do you notice about the water level here?" forces them to look and think.

That is how my aunties taught me. They never just told you the answer. They asked questions until you saw it yourself. I will do it that way. I was also thinking about giving each of them a notebook. Writing down what they observe each day on patrol.

A daily observation notebook is a great idea. It builds the habit of paying attention, and over weeks they'll be able to look back and see patterns they wouldn't have noticed otherwise. Do you keep one yourself? If they see you writing things down too, it signals that this is a real practice, not just a trainee exercise.

I still keep one after all these years. There is always something new to write down out here. Even after all this time on this wanga I notice things I have not seen before. That is what I want them to understand. You never stop learning the country.

That's a powerful lesson for them. One practical concern — the Melbourne ranger is going to struggle with the heat, especially in the first few weeks. People who haven't lived in tropical conditions often don't recognise heat stress in themselves until it becomes serious. It's worth being firm about hydration and sun protection from day one. Three litres of water minimum on patrol, hat on at all times.

The heat always catches the southern ones. I have seen it before. They think they can push through it but this country does not work that way. No shortcuts with the sun up here. I will be firm about it. Better to be strict early than deal with someone going down on patrol.

Exactly. And try to check in with each of them individually at the end of each week, at least for the first month. Even a short conversation — what they found difficult, what they're unsure about. New rangers sometimes won't speak up about struggling unless someone creates the space for it.

I will do that. Weekly check-ins. When I started here nobody checked in with me. I worked a lot of things out on my own. It was harder than it needed to be. These two will have it better. I want them to feel like they belong here. That takes time but it starts with someone showing you the way.

The fact that you remember what it was like without that support means you'll know exactly what they need. It sounds like you have a clear plan — observation phase, gradual handover, notebooks, question-based teaching, weekly check-ins, and being firm on safety basics. That's a solid framework.

When you put it like that it sounds more organised than it feels in my head. But that is the shape of it. I will start writing a few things down this week so I am ready when they arrive. Thanks for helping me think it through.

You clearly already know what good mentoring looks like from your own experience. Writing it down will just help you stay on track when the days get busy. Good luck with them — feel free to check in if anything comes up in the first few weeks.
